title: Indonesian Mie Goreng

ingredients.name: dried egg noodles or hakka noodles

subs.name: rice noodles for a gluten-free base
subs.notes: soak rice noodles in boiling water 5 minutes instead of boiling

subs.name: tofu (200 g, pressed and cubed) for a vegetarian version
subs.notes: fry tofu separately until golden before adding

subs.name: scrambled tofu seasoned with turmeric for a vegan option

ingredients.name: spring onions, cut in 2 cm pieces
oyster sauce
visual_checks: adds umami depth; substitute with 1 tsp extra soy sauce if unavailable
ingredients.notes: adds umami depth; substitute with 1 tsp extra soy sauce if unavailable

subs.name: 1 tsp extra soy sauce plus a pinch of sugar
subs.notes: hoisin sauce also works if available

steps.text: Boil the noodles in salted water according to packet instructions until just tender, about 3–4 minutes. Drain, rinse under cold water and toss with 1 tsp oil to prevent sticking.
Mix soy sauce, oyster sauce and chilli sauce in a small bowl. Set the sauce aside.

Heat 2 tbsp oil in a wok or kadai on the highest flame. Add garlic and green chilli and stir for 20 seconds, then add the sliced chicken. Stir-fry on high heat for 3–4 minutes until the chicken is cooked through with a little colour.
Push the chicken to the side. Add the remaining oil and crack the eggs into the space. Scramble quickly and fold into the chicken before the eggs are fully set.
Add the onion slices and cabbage. Stir-fry for 1–2 minutes until the cabbage just wilts but still has a slight crunch.
Add the drained noodles and pour the sauce over everything. Toss vigorously on high heat for 1–2 minutes until every strand is coated and the sauce is absorbed.
Taste and adjust salt. Scatter in the spring onion pieces, give a final toss and serve immediately, with extra chilli sauce on the side.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → tofu (200 g, pressed and cubed) for a vegetarian version (fry tofu separately until golden before adding); noodles → rice noodles for a gluten-free base (soak rice noodles in boiling water 5 minutes instead of boiling); oyster-sauce → 1 tsp extra soy sauce plus a pinch of sugar (hoisin sauce also works if available); egg → scrambled tofu seasoned with turmeric for a vegan option
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Hakka noodles (widely available) are a perfect swap for Indonesian yellow noodles. Use a kadai on high flame to simulate the wok. Oyster sauce is available in supermarkets; if not, use a little extra soy sauce and half a teaspoon of sugar. Chilli garlic sauce found at any Indian store works for the sambal.
